lede: Computers communicate using IP addresses, but humans prefer readable names like 'example.com'. The Domain Name System (DNS) is the phonebook that translates between the two. Whenever you visit a website, a frantic hidden search happens across the globe to find its address. Let's trace that search.

legend.title: Quick reference: Server Roles
legend.sub: The hierarchy of the DNS system.

rows.dataName: Usually run by your ISP. It does the hard work of asking other servers to find the IP.
The top of the hierarchy (.). Directs the Resolver to the appropriate TLD server.
Handles Top Level Domains like .com, .org. Directs the Resolver to the specific domain's server.
The final boss. Holds the actual IP address record for 'example.com'.

steps.title: The Browser asks the local Resolver
steps.desc: You type 'example.com' into your browser. Your computer asks its configured DNS Resolver (usually run by your Internet Service Provider): 'Hey, do you know the IP for example.com?'. If the Resolver doesn't have it cached, it starts a recursive search.

steps.title: Asking the Root Server
steps.desc: The Resolver first asks a Global Root Server. The Root doesn't know the exact IP for example.com, but it knows who handles '.com' domains. It replies: 'I don't know, but go ask the .com TLD Server at this IP.'

steps.title: Asking the TLD Server
steps.desc: The Resolver then goes to the .com Top Level Domain (TLD) Server. The TLD server says: 'I don't know the exact IP, but I know the Authoritative Name Server that manages example.com. Go ask them at this IP.'

steps.title: Asking the Authoritative Server
steps.desc: The Resolver finally contacts the Authoritative Name Server. This server is the ultimate source of truth for the domain. It checks its records and proudly replies: 'Yes, example.com is at 192.0.2.1!'

steps.title: Cached and Delivered
steps.desc: The Resolver saves (caches) the IP address so it doesn't have to do this whole dance again anytime soon. It then hands the IP address back to your Browser, allowing your Browser to finally make the actual HTTP connection to the website.
fields: Saved in Cache & Returned to Browser
